title: ‘Let’s have some more ambition’: Westpac boss calls for vision
full_text: Other Editions 06 May 2026 Business ‘Let’s have some more ambition’: Westpac boss calls for vision The Australian David Ross 6 May 2026 810 words English AUSTLN 15 © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. Westpac chief executive Anthony Miller has urged the Albanese government to articulate a vision for Australia, warning the country was beset by doomsayers and competing forces causing economic chaos. As Westpac ruled off its $3.4bn interim profits, Mr Miller said too much of the public discourse was “too negative”. He added that, while there were challenges, Australia had plenty of positives. But Westpac, one of Australia’s largest banks, is preparing for worse to come. The bank booked a $443m increase in its provisions, stashing cash in the event that fuel-exposed customers or households fall ­behind on loans amid a spike in ­energy costs. Westpac’s total provisions now stand at $5.2bn. “There is a lot of things Australia should be thinking about and very positively going after,” Mr Miller said. “Let’s have some more ambition for our country and more goals.” Mr Miller said artificial intelligence was a huge opportunity, saying it could be a “great unlock” and Australia could be a “real contributor to the AI ecosystem”. But Mr Miller said this had to be accompanied by an articulation of how people would benefit from AI. “This is not something that’s given to you,” he said. “This is something where you have to have the mindset that we’re going to see that through.” Westpac has continued growing its loan book over the first half of the financial year, despite exiting its former RAMS mortgage business. The bank’s mortgages were up 7 per cent, outpacing the broader banking system. Business lending also lifted 16 per cent, with Westpac noting a growing appetite for funding in agriculture, health and professional services. But Westpac’s net interest margin, the core measure of profitability on its lending, declined over the period to 1.89 per cent in March, from 1.95 per cent six months ­earlier. In a note to clients, UBS analyst John Storey said Westpac’s loans showed that the bank had prioritised loan growth over margins. “Credit quality remains front of mind for investors, and a key ­driver of expected financial performance, with Westpac topping up provisions through economic assumption changes,” Mr Storey said. Jarden analyst Matt Wilson echoed this, saying Westpac had “meaningfully accelerated its growth and risk appetite”, and this had driven its balance sheet and revenue base. Mr Wilson said the expansion in Westpac’s balance sheet may prove “ill-timed”, cautioning that it could be problematic should economic malaise from the Middle East spread to the credit cycle. “Howard Marks again rings in our ears. ‘The worst loans are made in the best of times’,” Mr Wilson noted. Westpac’s stressed exposures declined to 1.16 per cent of the loan book, despite the Iran conflict. Westpac said its overall loan book was well ahead of pre-Covid levels, with more customers ahead on loans. On Tuesday, the Reserve Bank moved to increase the cash rate a further 25 basis points, taking the borrowing rate to 4.35 per cent. Mr Miller said there were plenty of competing forces in the economy and, while increasing interest rates was aimed at putting pressure on consumer spending, the rising toll of energy prices could also bring about the same result, without the need for future rate rises. He pointed to the Albanese government’s decision to attempt to cut 160,000 people out of the NDIS. Mr Miller said the NDIS had been a key driver taking staff away from other areas. “It could help, but I’m not sure it’s the key unlock for inflation in the country,” he said. Mr Miller said he was concerned some business customers were delaying investment decisions amid the rate environment coupled with higher fuel prices. “The risk is that no decision today or a delayed decision today is, in effect, no investment opportunity and as a result, activity will fall off in that forward setting of three, six, 12 months,” he said. Mr Miller said builders needed confidence about pricing, pointing to a recent example in which a Westpac customer was faced with a massive price spike and supply uncertainty of piping for a major project. Mr Miller said Westpac’s UNITE program, which aims to slash duplication of technical systems or processes, was key to the bank boosting returns in years ahead. In March, Westpac migrated its advisers to the BT Panorama wealth platform. The bank has cut staff numbers over the past 12 months, now with almost 1000 fewer employees. Westpac will pay a 77c-a-share interim dividend on June 26, with $2.6bn to be paid out. Shares in Westpac closed down 2.3 per cent at $37.63, giving the company a market value of ­almost $129bn. summary: Westpac CEO Anthony Miller has called for a more ambitious vision for Australia, highlighting the need for positive public discourse amidst economic challenges. The bank reported a $3.4 billion interim profit but increased provisions by $443 million due to rising energy costs impacting customers. Miller emphasised the potential of artificial intelligence while cautioning about the economic environment affecting investment decisions.
publication:
Aussies reject AI – but it’s not for the reason you think
theaustralian.com.au04 May 2026 20:30 GMT783 wordsJared Lynch

Chief executives of Australia’s biggest companies and Anthony Albanese want us all to use more artificial intelligence-powered tools – but there is a big problem.
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Westpac's interim profit is $3.4 billion, with increased provisions of $443 million.
Miller advocates for a positive vision for Australia and highlights AI's potential.
Concerns over delayed business investments due to rising interest rates and energy costs.
relevance: Understanding the economic landscape and corporate strategies is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals in assessing risks and opportunities.
